title: China GP Reactions: Cashinho Penalty Overshadows Ferrari's Perfect Start
caption: The Ferrari garage — a complicated picture beneath the celebration.
body:
Eetu Väisänen won. Ferrari lead both championships. Two from two. And yet the conversation in the Shanghai paddock after the chequered flag was dominated not by the winner, but by the driver who cost himself dearly on the final lap.

Cachinho's Late Penalty — and the Six-Point Warning

In the closing stages of the Chinese Grand Prix, with the result long since decided at the front, Afonso Cachinho was observed making a forceful and poorly-judged move on Riley Swansson. Stewards reviewed the incident and handed Cachinho a five-second time penalty — a result that underlined a pattern that is rapidly becoming unreasonable to ignore. Across just two race weekends, Cashinho has accumulated six penalty points on his FIA racing license along with a formal warning. Six points in two rounds is a pace of accumulation that leads, if continued, to a race ban by mid-season. For a driver already under public performance scrutiny from his own team principal, the disciplinary dimension adds an entirely new layer of pressure.

Cachinho declined to comment extensively post-race. His engineers are understood to be working intensively on both his racecraft under pressure and his risk calibration in the final phases of events. The charitable reading is that a driver trying too hard for results in a compromised car position is taking decisions he normally wouldn't. The less charitable reading is forming in the paddock regardless.

Belacik: The Seat Conversation Continues

Ferrari Team Principal Randy Belacik, who had already warned after Melbourne that Cashinho's seat was not safe, gave another carefully worded but unmistakably pointed response after Shanghai. Two Q1 exits, no points scored, and now a penalty for avoidable contact are not results that Ferrari can contextualise as an adaptation period. Belacik has not publicly named a replacement, but the message is clear: Cashinho has a very short window to change the narrative.

Roy's Anger at the Pit Wall

Jason Roy's post-race manner was noticeably terse. Starting from pole, leading the opening phase, and then watching the race slip away through a pit stop call he himself did not endorse has left the Red Bull driver visibly frustrated. Roy is understood to have held a lengthy debrief with the Red Bull engineering group on Sunday evening, with the pit call under the early safety car the central point of contention. Red Bull's defence is that the free tyre window was too good to pass up. Roy's position — expressed bluntly, by all accounts — is that the team handed Ferrari the race on a plate. At P3 in the championship, he cannot afford for that to happen again.

Keisner: The Story Nobody Expected

If Roy left Shanghai frustrated, Robin Keisner left it with a growing recognition that might be the most unexpected development of the young season. A sprint podium on Saturday and a P5 race recovery from last on the grid on Sunday made him arguably the most complete two-day performer in the field outside Väisänen himself. The Audi driver said simply: "We knew the car could do more than people expected. We are doing more." That understated confidence, combined with actual results, is exactly how momentum builds in a long championship.

Väisänen: Two from Two, But No Complacency

The championship leader made his post-race media round brief. Two wins from two is not a position that requires extensive analysing in front of cameras — it requires protecting. Väisänen noted that the hard tyre strategy in Shanghai was the right call but that Japan would present a completely different challenge, with higher tyre loading and a track that exposes aerodynamic efficiency more honestly than the first two rounds. "We have a quick car. We have to keep it that way," he said. Ferrari, for now, are the standard against which every other team is measuring themselves.

Championship Picture After Two Rounds

Väisänen leads the Drivers' Championship comfortably. Kevu sits second, Ferrari lead the Constructors'. But the competitive picture is genuinely complex underneath: Racing Bulls are second in Constructors' standings, Keisner's Audi is punching well above its weight, and Springfield’s Aston Martin continues to surprise. Japan will be the first test on a track with enough high-speed load to definitively expose where the real performance margin lies. The paddock cannot wait.
